speaker: interviewer
text: The 5th of October, 1995. So, Stephanie, you said that two of the younger men pushed out a second board from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a whole-- just a tiny little--

speaker: interviewer
text: A second board from the first board.

speaker: interviewee
text: --bit of the second board. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and then what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then some youngsters did push-- not themselves. They got to pushed from the inside to the outside by the people who have still been inside, the young-- the young one who had-- who wanted to get out. And most of them just jumped. But by that time, it was already night, dark and rainy. And on the last carriage of the train, on the roof sat a Ukrainian with an automatic-- what do you call this gun which goes--?

speaker: interviewer
text: Machine gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: Machine gun. And he-- as soon as he heard some movement, he-- of course, he shot. Some made it. Others didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what happened to you? I got also pushed out by the people from inside to the outside. But I didn't jump. I hung on by my hands and went around-- went around the corner and let myself down in between the rails and laid down, let the train pass over me. And since it was dark and rainy and the Ukrainian has been shooting on the sides, never thinking that there could be somebody under the train. After the train went away, I got up. And where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been one station-- was it one or two stations?-- before Otwock, the line which I knew very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got up, went to the station, walked around. I have seen a station master. And he looked good. He looked like a decent Pole. And he was also from the resistance. So he's taking me to his little hut. I washed myself properly, because you can imagine laying under the train, and cleaned myself up and went back by train to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the flat.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the flat. And at the window of the flat, Vera has been sitting for the last three or four nights, waiting for me to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Vera left  the ghetto when in for the final time?

speaker: interviewee
text: She left shortly before the last aktion on my path.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your path. And in the period that you were in the ghetto going in and out, are there any specific events that you remember that you would like to tell us about?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the second aktion, I've stayed overnight on the Polish side and stupidly didn't ring to get to know if I can come in, if it's OK to come back into the ghetto, and just walked in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I add, sorry--

speaker: interviewee
text: --came to the gate--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I ask-- the telephones were not tapped-- no one was listening-- if you were given a warning on the phone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There haven't been very many phones in the ghetto. And the few people who had the phones it wasn't worth-- there have been a few phones. Real rich people had it and the one whom the Germans wanted to contact at any time and this sort of thing. And the Judenrat, they had phones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, you were saying you didn't call. You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came to the gate. And I didn't know that inside the ghetto, the aktion is there, that people are being rounded up and sent away. And the SS on the gate asked me, how do you come now? Where have you been all night? And I lied a little from the sky, where I have been and what have been doing, all those sort of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say? Do you remember what excuse you gave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes, that I had to deliver some papers and-- to the Germans. And it was too late to come back because the gates have been already closed. And he didn't believe me. And he said, oh, well, I will shoot you anyway just in case. And he tried to shoot, but his pistol has jammed twice. So he said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You know what that means?

speaker: interviewer
text: Devil?

speaker: interviewee
text: A devil sits in you. So instead of shooting, he grabbed his pistol by the-- by the wrong side.

speaker: interviewer
text: The muzzle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and hit me across the face with it. [LAUGHS] And I had a good blue mark. And I came home, which wasn't very far from the gate. And I have been asked by Vera, what happened to your face? I have been ashamed to say that I have been so stupid not to ring. Oh, I had a little accident. I never admitted that I got hit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any other images of the ghetto that will always stay with you? Are there things that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which-- what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any images of the ghetto that will always be with you, Stephanie? Is there anything else you can tell us about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I managed to put it out of my mind. Well, there are some things which come back. But you know, I'm not a person who will give in. So I say to myself, it's over, finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's nothing you'd like to add about the time that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we missed quite a few things. For example-- oh, that was really later. After the ghetto was liquidated and we lived on the Polish side in [NON-ENGLISH] Street, Grandma kept the house for us. Vera was afraid to put her nose out, because she looked more Jewish than I did. And she also didn't know how, but she acquired some Jewish accent. And that was also a very giveaway thing. And the kids, Roman and Eva, they got to this schools, but not real schools. They have been the trade schools. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Excuse me. They both went to trade schools, and they both did work. He worked in a metal work shop. While in ghetto, he attended the Judenrat, the [? council ?] school, too, this in the same-- in the same line, metal work. And Eva, she went to work in a little factory making paper bags. [LAUGHS] They both worked. And I went every day in the morning out, because I had a hundred little things to do and little shopping.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so Roman and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Eva were passing as Aryan children? Is that true?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mina-- that was Vera's daughter-- she was so blonde and blue-eyed that nobody would ever suspect even that she wouldn't be Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were passing as Aryan at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to adopt any special lifestyle to pass as an Aryan? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because we always lived in the Polish way. We never lived in the-- for example, we never spoke Yiddish. I don't know-- I didn't know a word of Yiddish till I came to the DP camp after the war. And that was something which wouldn't give us away. And my brother, Roman, he was not circumcised. That was also something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why how was he not circumcised?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my parents didn't believe in marking the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with Roman-- Roman and Eva, how old were they-- I mean, when they were at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they've been 10 when the war broke out. And he-- 10 and a bit. He was 15 or 14-- no, 14 and a half, not quite 15-- when we had bandits. You know, during the war there had been robberies [INAUDIBLE] just like there are now. And we had also such robberies-- robberies and bandits coming. And of course, they also suspected that they were Jewish. Well, the first thing they did to Roman-- put your pants down. But wasn't. They didn't know, whether you were to think if it is or it isn't. They grabbed a few things, like the [? portraits ?] and things which had been lying around, and made off.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so-- and so--

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: So Roman and Eva understood very definitely--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that it was a very serious situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my word. Kids are 14. They understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mina didn't. She was called Yasha-- [? Yamina. ?] So it was Yasha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did Eva and Roman adopt other names? Or did they then--

speaker: interviewee
text: The same as I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Roman [? Bockatski, ?] Eva [? Bockatska. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they kept their first names but not their family names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Vera's husband-- Mina didn't know that her father was shot and that he was dead. And we did tell, of course, all the neighbors and people we came in contact with that her husband has got work in-- as a part of Poland and he will only come now in the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, Stephanie, how did this group of women and children that you were part of-- how did you all support yourselves in this time? How did you feed yourselves once the Judenrat are gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, something came from selling off things which we still had, like valuable pictures or jewelry or-- and then I worked a bit, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were-- because you had the Kennkarte, did you-- could you draw  rations like a Pole?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [? My word. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you work in this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: For some time, I worked in [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was Warsaw city cleaning department. They've been sending out cleaning trucks with water in the streets and this sort of thing. Because my friends from the resistance-- they were there. So I got in there, too. But I got in only part time, not permanently. But it was enough to put on a front-- to put up a front that you have--

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the grandmother, Ida, had a Kennkarte, so she could--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Vera had nothing. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She got one.

speaker: interviewer
text: A false one.

speaker: interviewee
text: A false one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So she was then able to get food as a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- what were the living conditions like? I mean, what were the food-- what were your daily food and living--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't terribly good, but-- I remember going every two, three days into the market, and horse flesh-- horse meat, which is lovely, you know? Horse meat, when you make it properly, it's beautiful. I really got to like it. And vegetable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what news, if anything, through your resistance friends and so forth, did you have about what was happening to Jewry in Europe and what was happening with the course of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because the radios-- they all had-- the resistance people, they had the radios. We did know. Not only did we know, but we traced on the map how the Russian front is coming closer, where they are now and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know the enormity of what had happened to European Jewry at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you--?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what had happened in places like Auschwitz and Treblinka, the enormity of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or just did you know this was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I know. I have been in contact with my friends from the council, the Lichtenbaums. They'd been transported to the Lublin Ghetto. And they've been not straightaway put into the gas chambers, into Majdanek. But they've been kept in the arbeitslager, in the work camp. And I kept in contact with them through a driver from the Judenrat, from the council's car, a driver. When the council-- when the ghettos stopped existing, he was still a friend. And through him, I kept in contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did have contact with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: He drove to Lublin and got through his friends some message to the people. And I also got for the Lichtenbaums a flat. They had a housemate who was very loyal to them. I got the flat, and she kept it for them. But they didn't make it. They did not make it. And they didn't make it because as Maitec wrote to me, we can't come because we have two enormous, big, old packets, the two mothers. They didn't want to leave the mothers. That happened to more than to one family. They didn't want to separate. They wanted to leave the [INAUDIBLE] or the mothers, and they all perished. I've seen a woman from Kalisz, a Mrs. Praszcha, who pushed her boy, a little boy, maybe eight years. And he got taken away. And she remained. Some people did this. Others wouldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean she pushed the boy away?

speaker: interviewee
text: When there was aktion. She did let the child to be taken, but not herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did she do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she wanted to stay. She offered the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to her? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: She later was taken away, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Would you have any comments, Stephanie, on how the war and the ghetto situation affected women more than it affected men? In that women were usually carers and men--

speaker: interviewee
text: If it affected more the women than the men?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or no, not more-- no, not more--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a different way?

speaker: interviewer
text: In a different way. Were there things that happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that all depends what kind of people-- the thinking people, and that could have been women as well as men--

speaker: interviewer
text: The what? The thinking people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The thinking people, the people who--

speaker: interviewer
text: The intellectual people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, intellectual, who could see what's happening, have been affected in different ways. Some believed that they can make it, that they can get out of the whole situation. Others have been absolutely determined that they were not. I remember one-- the father of one of my friends when were marched into the wagons. He jumped out from the queue and faced the German with a rifle, who had been minding the  queue going into the wagons. And he said to him, shoot me now. And the German said, no, you're going to-- into the train. There have been people reacting differently.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, I suppose what I-- because it's fairly common that women were mainly the carers of the aged and the children, they-- was there any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't agree with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: The women had been working. They had to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And was there any social life in the ghetto? I mean, did you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning-- at the beginning yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, how old were you? How old were you, Stephanie, at this stage? 20? 20-ish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: 21 when--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any romance?

speaker: interviewee
text: No time for romance. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but was there a ghetto life--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there have been marriages. There have been romances. There have been marriages. Of course there have been. But not everybody has been so busy. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No, uh-uh. And when--

speaker: interviewee
text: And with other things on your mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And coming back to the time that you were now passing as an Aryan, you said you left the flat every day because you had a lot of little things to do. What were those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, what I wanted to tell you is that after a couple of years, Roman-- that was the boy-- he has decided after the bandits and after they have checked him out and all the rest of it, he has decided that he would like to get on by himself. Because he being not circumcised, he's fairly safe. But with all the other women and children, he may not be as well off. And Vera and I did not put our foot down. If you think that you will be safer by yourself, OK. We let him go. And he volunteered for work in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Into a camp where a German factory, who has been taking on laborers from-- because the Germans have been in the army. So they needed laborers from other nations. And he went into such a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whereabouts in Germany did he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kassel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Kassel is where? Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Kassel? Sorry. Near where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Westphalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Westphalia. And what was Vera's reaction to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we've talked, Vera and I. We talked about it quite some. We came to the conclusion that he's nearly 15, and if he really feels safer and better, how can we say no? If anything happens later, then it will be our responsibility. And he was old enough to look after himself. And he wanted it. How can you force somebody who feel safer by himself?

speaker: interviewer
text: So with all the women left, and what were these little things that you had to do every morning that you left the apartment for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes I worked in the cleaning department. And sometimes I went shopping. Sometimes I just went out to be out, to make the impression that you go, that you don't always stick around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. After those bandits came, we arranged for another-- for another safe house, where my friends from the resistance lived. And Vera, Mina, and Eva, the three, they went over there, got new Kennkarten. And grandma and I, we stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Changing names again? Or did they stay with the names that they had on the original Kennkarte?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they changed. They changed, And grandma and I, we stayed in the flat to see what happens. Oh, the mail just came in. Never mind. And a few days later-- two days later, not just a few-- just two days later, the same bandits-- I've been coming back from the market, and they found me and put a gun to my back and led me back to the flat. But I had not been going there. Just before the flat-- the entrance to the flat on the right-hand side-- there was a German [NON-ENGLISH]. I've been walking in front with a gun to the back. And when I came to the German [NON-ENGLISH], I didn't go forward, but I jumped to the side. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the man did fire the gun. The Germans from the [NON-ENGLISH] came out to see what's happening. And I told them, there was a man who went shooting me. And why would they want to shoot you? And this. And so-- and finally, they've taken my grandmother and myself into the Aleja Szucha. That was the Gestapo headquarters. And they said, you reported the bandits came there. All right. With my grandmother, who only spoke German and couldn't speak well Polish and with myself, we reported our incident, not saying that we've been suspected of being Jewish or anything like that. At that time-- So the bandits-- sorry-- were Poles, were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, it was very common in Warsaw, the robberies, like-- like it is here today, or even more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Stephanie, how many people actually knew your true identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends from the resistance, which have been still friends from Kalisz, from the time when we lived in Kalisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And none of them-- none of them gave you up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. Another queer coincidence-- as I told you before, my uncle was a president of the town. And has provided jobs for the caretaker's children in-- one was a message boy, and the other one was something else in the magistrate, as it was called, town hall. And we became real friends with his kids. And during the war, they came to Warsaw. And then there was another little-- another girl, who was a year behind me at school and whom I have been helping with her school work. And we also became friends. And they came to Warsaw. And they joined the Polish resistance. And they have been my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And these people--

speaker: interviewee
text: These people have taken care of all sort of things. And a coincidence-- this family, some of them have been left in Kalisz. But some, the older one, came into Warsaw. And one girl got taken by force to a-- a work camp in Germany to workers. And it was a camp to which Roman came to. And she recognized him, of course. And she said to me much later, after the war, I have seen correspondence coming to him. Roman was his name. And Eva was on the envelope, who has been writing it. Now, I had no more doubts it's the same Roman. And she helped him all through the time when they've been in the same workers' camp. But a coincidence of out-- out of all the workers' camps and out of all the peoples of different nationalities sent to the camps, these two kids came together again. He corresponds with her even now. When I needed a certificate, she gave it to me, only recently.

speaker: interviewer
text: And coming up to the uprising, the Warsaw uprising, what happened to you during and after the Warsaw uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the uprising, I've been in Warsaw itself. There was another Jewish man, also in the army. He was in the proper army, though he came across to me with his rifle on his back and gave me all the information, because I have not been in the shooting, so to say, army. I have been in the one which have just been gathering information. Anyway, the arising has died out, because Polish and Russian Army on the other side of the Vistula wouldn't come in while there was still a Warsaw. They waited till the Germans have finished off so that the Polish communist government could take over. And the people who survived have been marched out of the city into one of the stations-- railway stations past Warsaw. Because the railway station of Warsaw itself, the three railway stations, have been flattened. Well, and while we've been marched out, I skipped away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Skipped away? How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was towards evening, and there was a highway. And on both sides of the highways have been ditches, fairly deep ditches. So I hopped into one of those ditches.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of the ditch and into a building, which turned out to be a bakery. And this bakery has been supplying bread and rolls and all sort of things to the local villages-- not local, the surrounding villages, and, among other villages, the one where my sister and grandma and Eva and Mina have been. So I got with a bread cart and went back to my people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at one point had Eva-- Vera and the grandmother arrived at this village? Had they left before the uprising, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no-- well, way before the uprising. You see, when we lost the flat where the bandits came, we went for some time into a safe house where my friends, the resistance people, lived. But I felt it wasn't safe there. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Though we provide-- they provided for me in a village, real village, not any more little town or anything, a summer house. Because well-to-do people have been still going for the summer months out of the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went from their place into this place in [INAUDIBLE]. And one day I came back, because I had to come back to be in contact with the resistance. And they've been taken away from two places. Three people, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], and Vodak, have been from one place, where we stayed just before going to [INAUDIBLE]. And from another address in Warsaw, another two men-- they all were taken away by the Gestapo on the one day. And one survived. And I've met him in Kalisz after the war. All the others have never been seen again. And I asked him, when you've been arrested, when you've been taken away, what did they accuse you of, of hiding Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, no, there has never been any question. We've been taken to the Gestapo and a bit questioned, tortured, but not one question was about us helping or hiding any Jews. It was all only about the resistance. And we never gave any other names. We've been real friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this boy-- young man, I would say-- he got finished in Bergen-Belsen and was freed by the Russians, I think. Or was it American?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll just stop there. Thanks, thanks.
